audienceSections.normal: In Mia and Jonas meeting as neighbors, you will use [[grammar:Ich bin + N]]. Use [[grammar:Ich bin]] before your name or role. Ich bin Studentin means ‘I am a student.’ A profession has no article here, so do not add eine before Studentin.
audienceSections.expert: This chapter presents [[grammar:Ich bin + N]] in Mia and Jonas meeting as neighbors. The clause contains nominative subject ich, first-person singular finite copula bin, and a nominal predicate. Profession nouns are bare in this predicative use while retaining lexical gender.

audienceSections.normal: Use [[grammar:Ich bin]] before your name or role. Ich bin Studentin means ‘I am a student.’ A profession has no article here, so do not add eine before Studentin.
audienceSections.expert: The clause contains nominative subject ich, first-person singular finite copula bin, and a nominal predicate. Profession nouns are bare in this predicative use while retaining lexical gender.

audienceSections.normal: German nouns begin with capital letters. Learn each noun with der, die, or das, and watch where the finite verb goes in each sentence.
audienceSections.expert: The reading uses contemporary standard German. Noun citation forms retain nominative gender articles, while running text shows the case forms required by syntax.

breakdown.normal:
Read each exact german line, then check its natural English meaning. Watch for [[grammar:Ich bin + N]] where it appears.

- Hallo! — Hello!
- Guten Tag! — Good day!
- Ich bin Mia. — I am Mia.
- Ich bin Jonas, dein Nachbar. — I am Jonas, your neighbor.
- Ich bin Studentin. — I am a student.
- Schön, ich bin Bäcker. — Nice; I am a baker.
breakdown.expert:
